PDFs are great for sharing, but sometimes you need to lock them down. Resumes, contracts, financial statements — most of them contain information you don’t want just anyone to open.
Most free online tools make you upload your file to a remote server to add a password. For documents that sensitive, that’s a risk you shouldn’t take.
A browser-based alternative
Instead of uploading, you can password protect PDFs directly in your browser. The file stays on your device, nothing is sent to a server, and your password never leaves your computer.
Here’s how it works:

• Open the PDF Encrypt tool.

• Upload your PDF.

• Set a password.

• Click “Encrypt PDF” and download the locked file.
Common use cases

• Sending resumes — Protect the PDF so only the employer can open it

• Sharing financial documents — Bank statements, tax forms, anything with sensitive numbers

• Contract drafts — Before finalizing, share a version that only collaborators can open

• Student papers — Professors often require password-protected submissions

• Internal memos — Company policies, meeting notes, things that shouldn’t be forwarded
Limitations to know

• Open password only (requires password to open); doesn’t set permissions restrictions

• Strong passwords matter — at least 8 characters with letters, numbers, and symbols

• Don’t forget your password — there is no “forgot password” feature

• Very large PDFs may take a few seconds to encrypt
When this isn’t enough
Browser-based tools are great for quick password protection, but they have limits. If you need granular permissions (restrict printing, allow copying), digital signatures, or team access management, a desktop editor is the better long-term choice.
For users with heavier PDF workflows, Wondershare PDFelement is a solid option with advanced security features.
This article was originally published on SmartOn Tools.
